item.title: Illustrations from the Prodigiorum ac ostentorum chronicon (Chronicle of Prophec...
item.description:
🖼️ Slideshow (4 medier)

@archaeologyart

Illustrations from the Prodigiorum ac ostentorum chronicon (Chronicle of Prophecies and Omens). Author: Conrad Lycosthenes (born Conrad Wolffhart, 1518-1561). Publication date: August 1557. Printing technique: Woodcut. Collection: Multiple copies are held in the rare book collections of the Getty Research Institute, the Bibliothèque interuniversitaire de santé (Paris), and the Australian Museum Research Library.

The book's author, Conrad Lycosthenes, was born Conrad Wolffhart in 1518 in the town of Rouffach, Alsace. He translated his name into Greek, becoming *Lycosthenes*. After studying philosophy in Heidelberg, he moved to Basel, where he taught grammar and dialectics for over a decade while also serving as a deacon at St. Leonard's Church. So, the author was a clergyman, an academic, and a nature enthusiast all at once.

The full title of the Prodigiorum translates to -The Chronicle of Omens and Prophecies that have occurred in both the upper and lower regions of the earth, outside the order, motion, and operation of nature, from the beginning of the world to the present day.- This wasn't merely a compilation; it was a comprehensive inventory of all the bizarre, uncanny, and extraordinary events spanning from the time of Adam and Eve to the year 1557.

In a preface, Lycosthenes explains that these events "symbolize God's wrath toward humanity and momentous transformations in the world". In Reformation-era Germany and Switzerland, every comet was a warning, and every lunar eclipse was a potential sign of punishment. This mindset had developed into such a widespread preoccupation at the time that, according to historian Richard Barnes, by 1560, the fixation on extraordinary natural phenomena had practically become an obsession. Lycosthenes's book was a natural product of this cultural climate.

Se slideshow på Instagram
item.content:encoded:
🖼️ Slideshow (4 medier)

@archaeologyart

Illustrations from the Prodigiorum ac ostentorum chronicon (Chronicle of Prophecies and Omens). Author: Conrad Lycosthenes (born Conrad Wolffhart, 1518-1561). Publication date: August 1557. Printing technique: Woodcut. Collection: Multiple copies are held in the rare book collections of the Getty Research Institute, the Bibliothèque interuniversitaire de santé (Paris), and the Australian Museum Research Library.

The book's author, Conrad Lycosthenes, was born Conrad Wolffhart in 1518 in the town of Rouffach, Alsace. He translated his name into Greek, becoming *Lycosthenes*. After studying philosophy in Heidelberg, he moved to Basel, where he taught grammar and dialectics for over a decade while also serving as a deacon at St. Leonard's Church. So, the author was a clergyman, an academic, and a nature enthusiast all at once.

The full title of the Prodigiorum translates to -The Chronicle of Omens and Prophecies that have occurred in both the upper and lower regions of the earth, outside the order, motion, and operation of nature, from the beginning of the world to the present day.- This wasn't merely a compilation; it was a comprehensive inventory of all the bizarre, uncanny, and extraordinary events spanning from the time of Adam and Eve to the year 1557.

In a preface, Lycosthenes explains that these events "symbolize God's wrath toward humanity and momentous transformations in the world". In Reformation-era Germany and Switzerland, every comet was a warning, and every lunar eclipse was a potential sign of punishment. This mindset had developed into such a widespread preoccupation at the time that, according to historian Richard Barnes, by 1560, the fixation on extraordinary natural phenomena had practically become an obsession. Lycosthenes's book was a natural product of this cultural climate.

Se slideshow på Instagram
item.author: archaeologyart

item.title: The Journey Home. Artist: Tommi Nordgren @tomminordgrenart. (Finland), 2014. Med...
item.description:
@archaeologyart

The Journey Home. Artist: Tommi Nordgren @tomminordgrenart. (Finland), 2014. Medium: Oil on canvas, 81 x 100 cm.

Tommi Nordgren was born in Helsinki in 1974. He studied at the Pekka Halonen Academy of Art and later at the St. Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts. For nearly a decade, he's lived and worked in Belyakovets, a small village near Veliko Tarnovo, Bulgaria.

In European cultural tradition, the black cat has carried a complex and contradictory symbolic weight since the Middle Ages. The Christian church, especially as witch hunts escalated in the 16th century, transformed the black cat first into the devil's messenger, and eventually into the devil's physical embodiment.

But the black cat's symbolic history hasn't ever been one-dimensional. The Egyptians held cats sacred. In British and Irish traditions, it's still considered good luck today if a black cat crosses your path. In Northern European and Scandinavian folklore, the cat is the guardian and protector of the home; its rooftop prowls were seen as keeping watch against evil spirits.
item.content:encoded:
@archaeologyart

The Journey Home. Artist: Tommi Nordgren @tomminordgrenart. (Finland), 2014. Medium: Oil on canvas, 81 x 100 cm.

Tommi Nordgren was born in Helsinki in 1974. He studied at the Pekka Halonen Academy of Art and later at the St. Petersburg Academy of Fine Arts. For nearly a decade, he's lived and worked in Belyakovets, a small village near Veliko Tarnovo, Bulgaria.

In European cultural tradition, the black cat has carried a complex and contradictory symbolic weight since the Middle Ages. The Christian church, especially as witch hunts escalated in the 16th century, transformed the black cat first into the devil's messenger, and eventually into the devil's physical embodiment.

But the black cat's symbolic history hasn't ever been one-dimensional. The Egyptians held cats sacred. In British and Irish traditions, it's still considered good luck today if a black cat crosses your path. In Northern European and Scandinavian folklore, the cat is the guardian and protector of the home; its rooftop prowls were seen as keeping watch against evil spirits.
item.author: archaeologyart

item.title: Comic Bats (Yatsuatari dōke kōmori). Artist: Utagawa Kuniyoshi, circa 1846. Medi...
item.description:
@archaeologyart

Comic Bats (Yatsuatari dōke kōmori). Artist: Utagawa Kuniyoshi, circa 1846. Medium: Polychrome woodblock print. Source / Collection: Richard Kruml - JapanesePrints-London.

In the early 1840s, the Tokugawa shogunate passed strict censorship laws known as the Tenpō Reforms to stop luxury spending in society. The government banned ukiyo-e artists from drawing popular kabuki theater actors. Artists who broke these rules faced heavy penalties, including the destruction of their woodblocks. To get around the censorship, Kuniyoshi found a solution in humor. He began drawing the banned actors as cats, frogs, and bats. The people of Edo instantly recognized their favorite actors from the clothes and specific patterns these human-like animals wore.

The artwork I'm sharing is a parody of Sukeroku Yukari no Edo Zakura, one of the most famous kabuki plays of its time. The cherry blossoms in the background serve as a visual nod to the phrase "Edo Cherry Blossoms" in the play's title. The bat on the left, with its purple headband and black appearance, plays the main character, Sukeroku. The white-bearded figure on the right takes on the role of the story's main villain, the old samurai Ikyu. In the original play, Sukeroku tries to provoke Ikyu into drawing his sword, hoping to find his stolen family heirloom sword. To anger him, he deliberately holds his pipe between his toes and offers it to him.

In Japanese culture, it's an unacceptable insult to offer someone an object with your foot. The bat presenting the smoking accessory with its foot captures that iconic moment of provocation from the stage. The artist used his artistic cleverness to bypass the government bans and continued sharing the era's beloved theater scenes with the public.
item.content:encoded:
@archaeologyart

Comic Bats (Yatsuatari dōke kōmori). Artist: Utagawa Kuniyoshi, circa 1846. Medium: Polychrome woodblock print. Source / Collection: Richard Kruml - JapanesePrints-London.

In the early 1840s, the Tokugawa shogunate passed strict censorship laws known as the Tenpō Reforms to stop luxury spending in society. The government banned ukiyo-e artists from drawing popular kabuki theater actors. Artists who broke these rules faced heavy penalties, including the destruction of their woodblocks. To get around the censorship, Kuniyoshi found a solution in humor. He began drawing the banned actors as cats, frogs, and bats. The people of Edo instantly recognized their favorite actors from the clothes and specific patterns these human-like animals wore.

The artwork I'm sharing is a parody of Sukeroku Yukari no Edo Zakura, one of the most famous kabuki plays of its time. The cherry blossoms in the background serve as a visual nod to the phrase "Edo Cherry Blossoms" in the play's title. The bat on the left, with its purple headband and black appearance, plays the main character, Sukeroku. The white-bearded figure on the right takes on the role of the story's main villain, the old samurai Ikyu. In the original play, Sukeroku tries to provoke Ikyu into drawing his sword, hoping to find his stolen family heirloom sword. To anger him, he deliberately holds his pipe between his toes and offers it to him.

In Japanese culture, it's an unacceptable insult to offer someone an object with your foot. The bat presenting the smoking accessory with its foot captures that iconic moment of provocation from the stage. The artist used his artistic cleverness to bypass the government bans and continued sharing the era's beloved theater scenes with the public.
item.author: archaeologyart

item.title: Guardian Angel. Ivan Leonidovich Lubennikov (Minsk, Belarus, May 14, 1951 - Mosc...
item.description:
@archaeologyart

Guardian Angel. Ivan Leonidovich Lubennikov (Minsk, Belarus, May 14, 1951 - Moscow, Russia, October 3, 2021). Date: 2001. Medium: Oil on canvas.
item.content:encoded:
@archaeologyart

Guardian Angel. Ivan Leonidovich Lubennikov (Minsk, Belarus, May 14, 1951 - Moscow, Russia, October 3, 2021). Date: 2001. Medium: Oil on canvas.
item.author: archaeologyart

item.title: The Weeping Eye Emblem from the Minerva Britanna, or A Garden of Heroical Deuise...
item.description:
@archaeologyart

The Weeping Eye Emblem from the Minerva Britanna, or A Garden of Heroical Deuises. Author: Henry Peacham, 1612. Technique: Woodcut. Collection: Folger Shakespeare Library, Washington D.C.

Minerva Britanna is considered the most sophisticated English-language example of a genre that began in 16th-century Europe with Andrea Alciato's Emblemata (1531) and saw hundreds of thousands of copies printed over the next century. Each emblem has three basic layers: The first is the Superscriptio (motto): usually a short, concise Latin title. Known as the soul of the emblem, this verbal element guides the reader to the right interpretation.

The second is the Pictura: an allegorical woodcut illustration. It's the "body" of the emblem; rather than being purely formal, it serves a symbolic and didactic purpose.

The third is the Subscriptio: the English metrical text interpreting the emblem, usually made up of two six-line stanzas. It grounds the meaning created by the image and motto in the social and moral context of the time.
This eye is a pars pro toto, meaning a part chosen to represent the whole. By placing the eye independently from a body, it's transformed from a symbol of personal suffering into a representation of an abstract truth - surveillance, mourning, or fate.

So, what's being conveyed here?

"Hei mihi quod vidi." - This phrase sits at the top of the page.

It essentially translates to "Woe is me for what I've seen!" or "Alas for what I saw!"

This is a reference to Genesis III: the loss of innocence and Original Sin. Adam and Eve's eyes opening after eating the fruit from the Tree of Knowledge, and realizing their nakedness, is humanity's first and greatest "Hei mihi quod vidi" moment. In this context, the opening of the eye isn't just a biological act, but a tragic realization; it's forbidden knowledge lifting the veil of ignorance, leaving shame, mortality, and sorrow in its wake.

(1/2) Continued Below (1/2)
item.content:encoded:
@archaeologyart

The Weeping Eye Emblem from the Minerva Britanna, or A Garden of Heroical Deuises. Author: Henry Peacham, 1612. Technique: Woodcut. Collection: Folger Shakespeare Library, Washington D.C.

Minerva Britanna is considered the most sophisticated English-language example of a genre that began in 16th-century Europe with Andrea Alciato's Emblemata (1531) and saw hundreds of thousands of copies printed over the next century. Each emblem has three basic layers: The first is the Superscriptio (motto): usually a short, concise Latin title. Known as the soul of the emblem, this verbal element guides the reader to the right interpretation.

The second is the Pictura: an allegorical woodcut illustration. It's the "body" of the emblem; rather than being purely formal, it serves a symbolic and didactic purpose.

The third is the Subscriptio: the English metrical text interpreting the emblem, usually made up of two six-line stanzas. It grounds the meaning created by the image and motto in the social and moral context of the time.
This eye is a pars pro toto, meaning a part chosen to represent the whole. By placing the eye independently from a body, it's transformed from a symbol of personal suffering into a representation of an abstract truth - surveillance, mourning, or fate.

So, what's being conveyed here?

"Hei mihi quod vidi." - This phrase sits at the top of the page.

It essentially translates to "Woe is me for what I've seen!" or "Alas for what I saw!"

This is a reference to Genesis III: the loss of innocence and Original Sin. Adam and Eve's eyes opening after eating the fruit from the Tree of Knowledge, and realizing their nakedness, is humanity's first and greatest "Hei mihi quod vidi" moment. In this context, the opening of the eye isn't just a biological act, but a tragic realization; it's forbidden knowledge lifting the veil of ignorance, leaving shame, mortality, and sorrow in its wake.

(1/2) Continued Below (1/2)
item.author: archaeologyart
